author_background: Former marine biologist and university professor specializing in coral reef ecosystems. Married to Lena with two daughters - Maya (8) and Chloe (6). Was working on a groundbreaking study about thermal adaptation in tropical corals when the outbreak began. Dreamed of establishing marine conservation zones and taking his family sailing around the world.
location: Abandoned coastal research station, Pacific Northwest - perched on cliffs overlooking a perpetually gray ocean. The building groans with each gust of wind, salt crusting every window. Faded marine charts still cling to the walls, their corners curling like dead leaves.
entry:
Day 187. The rain hasn't stopped for three days. It drums against the corrugated metal roof in a rhythm that's become the soundtrack to my madness. I found Lena's favorite hair clip today, tucked in the back of a drawer I'd been using for kindling. The little silver dolphin she always wore, even when dressing up. My hand trembled so badly I nearly dropped it. I can still hear Maya's voice in the patter of rain - 'Daddy, when can we go to the aquarium again?' I have to stop writing when I think of them. The ink smears. It's not just the damp. 

Yesterday, I had to make a choice that would have horrified the man I was before. A young woman stumbled into the perimeter - maybe early twenties, bleeding from a bite on her arm. She begged for help, her eyes wide with that particular terror I've come to recognize. The fever was already setting in. I had my rifle. I had the safety off. She saw the conflict in my eyes and whispered 'Please' just once. In the end, I couldn't do it. I led her to the old boathouse, left water and antibiotics, and locked the door from the outside. This morning, the scratching had stopped. Another ghost to join the chorus in my head. 

Sometimes I stand at the observation deck and watch the waves. The ocean hasn't changed. The gulls still cry, the tides still turn. There's comfort in that permanence, even as everything else has turned to ash and memory. I talk to Lena sometimes, tell her about the small victories - a successful fishing trip, finding unspoiled rainwater. She doesn't answer, but the habit keeps me anchored.
condition: Physically: Malnourished (down 15kg), persistent cough from damp conditions, left knee stiffens in the cold. Mentally: Suffering from complex grief, experiences auditory hallucinations of family members' voices, sleeps in 2-3 hour intervals, jumps at unexpected sounds.
discoveries: The infected show aversion to saltwater - they'll wade in if chasing prey but quickly retreat. Heavy rainfall masks human scent significantly. Canned goods with damaged seals can be salvaged if boiled for 20+ minutes. The change isn't viral - it's something fungal, spreading through airborne spores during certain weather conditions.
warnings: Never travel during heavy fog - spore concentration increases dramatically. Avoid coastal areas during red tide events - the toxins affect humans but seem to attract the infected. Trust no one who claims to have a 'cure' - I've seen what happens at those supposed safe zones. Boil all water, even if it looks clean.
last_meal: Canned sardines (found in station pantry) and rehydrated seaweed gathered from rocks below the station - eaten approximately 6 hours ago
companions: Currently alone. Lost wife Lena and daughters Maya/Chloe during evacuation day 14. Briefly traveled with former station caretaker (Marcus) days 45-89 until he sacrificed himself during a horde attack to give me time to escape.
